content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 22, 2010

Honorable Dennis Hollingsworth
State Capitol, Room 305
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-133

Dear Senator Hollingsworth:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Will reimbursements for travel and lodging from the American Legislative Exchange
Council's ("ALEC") scholarship fund in connection with ALEC's annual legislative meeting
constitute gifts made to public officials even if ALEC is a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization? If
so, are those payments reportable by ALEC, by the private donors who fund the scholarship
fund, or both?

2. If ALEC scholarship funds are solicited and raised by a member of the California
Legislature, what reporting consequences follow from that solicitation for that member and for a
member who receives reimbursement for travel and lodging expenses from ALEC scholarship
funds? Would the analysis change if the member involved in soliciting donations was also
involved in making recommendations to ALEC as to which members of the California
Legislature should receive ALEC scholarship reimbursements for travel and lodging for ALEC's
annual legislative meeting?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 2

3. Are any scholarship funds raised prior to June 10, 2010, the effective date of recently
amended Regulation 18945, subject to the intermediary gift disclosure described in the new
version of the Regulation?

4. May a legislator use campaign funds to cover costs to attend an ALEC function or
meeting?

CONCLUSIONS

1. If ALEC scholarship fund donors know or have reason to know that the sole or
primary purpose of their donations is to make gifts to California legislators the donations would
be reportable gifts to legislators from the donors to the fund and each donor's pro rata share
would be subject to the Act's $420 gift limit. In such a transaction ALEC would be an
intermediary of the gift and would need to disclose the sources of the funds it used to make
scholarships and other information as specified below to the recipients of those scholarships. In
addition, the recipients would be required to report those donors as sources of gifts on their
Statements of Economic Interests if the share of the donation from any one donor was $50 or
more in a calendar year. The legislators may also be disqualified from making, participating in
making, or using their official positions to influence the outcome of governmental decisions
involving those donors, as discussed below. However, you stated that ALEC is a large
organization that provides scholarships to public officials in many states. If the donors making a
donation to ALEC do not know or have reason to know that their donations will be solely or
primarily used for scholarships to California legislators, then ALEC is the source of the gifts and
not the individual donors.

2. If a California legislator solicits a donation to ALEC for scholarship funds and the
donor knows or has reason to know that the purpose of the donation is to pay for a scholarship
for the legislator, the donor is the source of the gift, and ALEC is an intermediary. Also, as
explained below, depending on what is communicated between the donor and ALEC in
connection with the making of the donation, the donation could be a gift to the legislator or other
legislators who received a scholarship, making the donor the source of the gift and ALEC the
intermediary. In addition, regardless of the solicitation by the legislator, the donor could be the
source of the gift and ALEC the intermediary as set forth in the analysis of Question 1 above.
Finally, if, in response to the California legislator's solicitation, a donor makes a donation or
several donations to the scholarship fund and the amount of the donations is $5,000 or more in a
calendar year, as explained below, the legislator must file a "behested payment report" (FPPC
Form 803) with his or her house of the Legislature within 30 days after the donations from a
single donor reach the $5,000 threshold. A behested payment report should also be filed by the
legislator who makes recommendations to ALEC as to which California legislators should
receive the scholarships listing ALEC as the payor and the recipient legislator as the payee when
the payments by ALEC reach the $5,000 threshold.

3. Scholarship funds raised prior to June 10, 2010, the effective date of amendments to
Regulation 18945, are not subject to the new version of that regulation.

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 3

4. Yes, legislators may use their campaign funds to cover costs to attend ALEC functions
or meetings.

FACTS

You are the Republican Leader of the California State Senate and are writing on behalf of
ALEC. ALEC is a nonprofit voluntary membership organization of over 1,700 state legislators
and nearly 300 private members, foundations and associations. ALEC is organized and exempt
as a charitable organization under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code and has been
since 1975. The primary purpose of ALEC is to educate legislators and private sector members
on the issues that affect state government and to develop solutions to those issues. The
organization engages in research, creation of model legislation and best practices for state
legislatures. ALEC also has discussions regarding public issues and accomplishes its
educational goals by publishing its findings and sponsoring educational seminars and
conferences.

ALEC has members and participation from all 50 states and all 99 state legislative
chambers. ALEC receives private donations into its scholarship fund that are not earmarked for
a particular purpose. From this fund ALEC offers scholarships at its own discretion to the
California Legislature to attend its annual legislative meeting, at which legislative education and
issues of state and national policy are discussed. The scholarships are offered to reimburse the
legislators' travel and lodging for attendance at ALEC seminars and conferences. At ALEC's
national chairman appoints a California state legislator to serve as a public sector chair. The
state chair serves as the head of the delegation and has discretion to recommend, and without
consultation with those being reimbursed or the donors to the fund, which members of the
California Legislature should be reimbursed for their travel and lodging expenses. Neither the
private nor the public sector members are involved in making determinations on reimbursements
except for the one state public sector chair that makes recommendations to ALEC.

ANALYSIS

Making and Receipt of Gifts

The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as:

"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."²

The Act regulates the receipt of gifts by legislators and specific legislative staff in four
ways that are relevant to your question.

_______________________
² Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any "rendering of . . . services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible."

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 4

• First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. Certain state and local officials are prohibited from accepting gifts from any single source in any calendar year with a total value of more than the gift limit. (Section 89503(a).) The current gift limit, which is adjusted biennially each odd number year to reflect changes in the Consumer Price Index, is $420. (Regulation 18940.2.)

• Second, the Act prohibits lobbyists and lobbying firms from making gifts to a legislator or specific legislative staff aggregating more than $10 in a calendar month, or to act as an agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift by any other person. (Section 86203.)

• Third, so that the public is made aware of any potential undue influences from donors who make gifts, the Act imposes reporting obligations requiring certain public officials to disclose the source of any gifts aggregating $50 or more in value. Section 87203 provides that "[e]very person who holds an office specified in Section 87200 shall . . . file a statement disclosing his [or her] investments, his [or her] interests in real property and income." The term "income" includes any gift. (Section 82030.) Elected State Officers, including Members of the Legislature, are included in Section 87200. (Section 82024.)

• Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $420 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Section 87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4; as to conflicts of interest and members of the Legislature, also see Sections 87102, 87102.5 and 87102.6.)

Notwithstanding the general gift provisions explained above, Section 89506 controls whether a gift of travel is subject to the Act's gift limit, providing the following in pertinent part:

"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence that are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of the following apply:

* * *

"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status under Section

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 5

501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code."

Question 1

Based upon the facts you have provided, the travel in question appears to be reasonably
related to an issue of state, national, or international public policy in that the travel will give
officials information on issues related to their work in the California Legislature. If the source of
any payment for the travel is ALEC, which is a nonprofit organization exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, the payment is not subject to the Act's
$420 gift limit.³ If the true source of the gifts is not one of the entities set forth in Section
89506(a)(2), the gift limit would apply.

Source of Gift

Notwithstanding, the gift exception for travel payments set forth above, you have
indicated that the payments for the travel and lodging will come from ALEC's scholarship fund,
which is funded by private donors. Thus, you must determine whether the source of any
particular payment is ALEC or the donor making a payment to ALEC for the scholarship fund.
Recently amended Regulation 18945 provides the Commission's rule for determining the source
of a gift and provides the following, in pertinent part:

"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a gift to an
official and is not acting as an intermediary.

"(b) Gifts through an Intermediary.

"(1) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 87210 or 87313, if the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirectly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the following conditions:

"(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party to make a gift to the official.

"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment will be used
by the third party to make a gift to the official.

"(C) The person identifies the official to the third party as the intended

³ While a payment for travel by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization is not subject to the $420 gift limit, any
payments totaling $50 or more on a calendar year must be disclosed as a reportable gift under Section 87203.
Moreover, the payment may bar the public official who received the payment from making, or influencing government decisions affecting ALEC, if they were the source of the gift. Even if a gift of travel
from a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization is exempt from the $420 gift limit, the official generally must still disqualify
himself or herself from any governmental decision with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
participation in the government decision. (Section 87100 et seq.)

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 6

beneficiary of the payment prior to the third party making the payment to the
official.

"(D) The third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to the third
party.

"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
purpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials.

"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official."

Based on your facts, the provision that seems most applicable to your first question would be
subdivision (E). Subdivision (E) focuses on the "sole or primary" purpose for making a
payment, necessarily based on the objective facts and circumstances that evidence an intent of
the donor. In Commission Memorandum concerning the adoption of
Regulation 18945 (Amendment to Regulation 18945 – Source of Gifts, March 17, 2010), staff
provided the following continuum:

"For example, a person may make a payment to a third person specifically for an
identified conference knowing all attendees will be state legislators and that the
payment will ultimately pay for the legislators' travel and accommodations. In
these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party should be
considered the source of the gifts.

[¶]

"On the other hand, if a person makes a payment for a widely attended event,
there is little indication that the payment will be used to make a gift to an official
even when the person knows or has reason to know that the official will attend the
event. In these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party
should not be considered the source of a gift provided to the official from the third
party hosting the event."

Based on this, if the donors to ALEC's scholarship fund know or have reason to know
that the sole or primary purpose of their donations is to make gifts to California legislators, then
the true sources of the scholarship funds dispersed by ALEC to the legislators are the donors to
the ALEC scholarship fund, and not ALEC. ALEC would be the intermediary of the gifts.¹

It appears likely that at least some donors to the ALEC scholarship fund know or have

¹ Under Sections 87210 and 87313, an intermediary of a gift to an official is required to provide the
following specific information to the officer: the name, address and business activity of both the intermediary and
donor. In addition, since the value of the gift in this case is likely to be a pro rata share of the donor's total donation
to ALEC, it would also be helpful to provide the official with that value.

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 7

reason to know that their contributions will fund the costs associated with the attendance of
California legislators to ALEC events. As you stated, ALEC regularly provides reimbursements
for travel and lodging to members of the California Legislature through its scholarship fund and
your questions indicate that these funds are sometimes solicited by California legislators.
However, because ALEC is a large organization that provides scholarships to public officials
from many states, the donors may not know or have no reason to know that their donations
would be solely or primarily used for scholarships to California legislators. In that case, ALEC
would be the source of the gift and not the individual donors to the scholarship fund.

Question 2

In your second question, you ask what reporting consequences follow for a legislator if
scholarship funds are solicited and raised by a legislator for the legislator who solicited the
funds, for a legislator who receives such funds, and for a legislator who makes recommendations
to ALEC as a public sector chair regarding which legislators should receive the scholarship
funds. Subdivision (b)(1)(F) of Regulation 18945, cited above, provides that when an official
solicits payment from a donor to a third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official, the
donor is the source of the gift to that official and the third party the intermediary if the official
receives a gift responsive to the solicitation within 12 months of making the solicitation. (See
also Regulation 18945(d)(3).) Therefore, to the extent the California legislator, or his or her
agent, solicits donations to the scholarship fund, and indicates that the California legislator will
be the beneficiary, the donor is the source of the gift and ALEC is an intermediary.

In addition, depending upon what is communicated as part of the solicitation or between
the donor and ALEC in connection with the donation, subdivision (b)(1)(A)-(D) could apply.
Under subdivision (b)(1)(A)-(D), the donor is the source of the gift to the legislator or other
benefitting legislators and ALEC the intermediary.5 Finally, even if subdivisions (b)(1)(A)-(D),
or (F) do not apply, if the facts surrounding the solicitation and making of the donation are
similar to those analyzed under Question 1 above, subdivision (b)(1)(E) may apply and the donor
could be the source of the gift and ALEC the intermediary.

Also, Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) states that when a payment for a legislative,
governmental or charitable purpose is made "at the behest" of an elected official, that official is
required to file a report with his or her agency within 30 days detailing, among other things, the
source, amount and purpose of the payment once the amount or amounts donated at the behest of
that official by a single source total to $5,000 or more in a calendar year. This is known as a
"behested payment report" and the Commission's Form 803 is used for that purpose. The public
sector chair who makes recommendations to ALEC as to which legislators should receive the
scholarship funds should also file behested payment reports for the payments made by ALEC to the
legislators due to his or her recommendations that reach $5,000 or more. On these reports ALEC

5 For example, under subdivision (b)(1)(C), if the donor sends a check to ALEC and communicates to
ALEC that the donation is to assist in sending that California legislator, or any other California legislators, named or
un-named, to an ALEC event, the donor would be the source of the gift to the legislators who benefit from the
payment, and ALEC the intermediary. (See Regulation 18945(b)(2) for clarification of how an official can be
identified so as to trigger application of these provisions.)

File No. A-10-133
Page No. 8

should be listed as the payor and the legislator who receives the recommended payment would
be the payee. It should also be noted that Legislators who receive gifts through the ALEC
scholarship fund may be disqualified from making, participating in making, or using their
official positions to influence the outcome of governmental decisions that involve the donors
who were sources of gifts to them. (Sections 87100, 87102, 87102.5 and 18702.6.)

Question 3

In response to your third question, because Regulation 18945 went into effect on June 10,
2010, any funds raised by ALEC prior to June 10, 2010, would only be subject to the prior
version of Regulation 18945.

Question 4

Finally, in response to your fourth question, legislators are permitted to use their
campaign funds to cover costs to attend ALEC's functions or meetings. The Act provides that
campaign funds may be used for expenditures associated with holding office if the expenditures
are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. Expenditures which confer a
substantial personal benefit must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Section 89512.) Therefore, because attendance at ALEC's annual legislative meeting
is related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose legislators may use their campaign
funds to cover costs to attend ALEC's functions or meetings. Also, please note that legislators
should refer to Regulation 18421.7 for additional reporting guidelines when reporting
expenditures for travel and meals made with campaign funds.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Commission Counsel
Legal Division

more in a calendar year' The legislators may_also be disqualified from making, participating in
!*inq, or using their official positions to influence the outcome of govemmental decisions
involving those donors, as discussed below. However, you stated th;t ALEC is a large
organization that provides scholarships to public officiais in many states. If the donors making a

primarily used for scholarships to california legislators, then ALEC is the source of the gifts and
not the individual donors.
2' lf a california legislator solicits a donation to ALEC for scholarship funds and the .
donor knows or has reason to know that the purpose of the donation is to pay for a scholarship
for the legislator, the donor is the source of tire gift, and ALEC is an interm"oi*y. Also, as
explained below, depending on what is communicated between the donor and ALEC in
connection with the making of the donation, the donation could be a gift to the legislator or other
legislators who received a scholarship, making the donor the source of the gift and ALEC the
intermediary' In addition, regardlesJof the so'iicitation by the legislator, the donor could be the
xource of the gift and ALEC the intermediary as set forth in the analysis of euestion I above.
Finally, if, in response to the califomia legisiator's solicitation, a donor makes a donation or
several donations to lh: schorarship fund and the amount of the donations is $5,000 or more in a
calendar year, as explained below,lhe Iegislator must file a "behested payment report,, (Fppc

Regulation 18945, are not subject to the new version of that regulation.


You are the Republican Leader of the Califomia State Senate and are writing on behalf of
ALEC' ALEC is a nonprofit voluntary membership organization of over 1,700 state legislators
and nearly 300 private members, foundations and assoc'iations. ALEC i, o.gonir.d and exempt
as a charitable organization under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue code and has been
since 1975' The primary purpose of ALEC is to educate legislators and private sector members
. on the issues that affect state government and to develop solutions to those issues. The
organization engages in research, creation of model legislation and best practices for state
legislatures. ALEC also has discussions regarding puiti" issues ona o".i.piirrr., it,
educational goals by publishing its findings-and sfonsoring educational seminars and
conterences.
ALEC has members and participation from all 50 states and all 99 state legislative
chambers' ALEC receives private donitions into its scholarship fund that are not earmarked for
a particular purpose. From this fund ALEC offers scholarships at its own discretion to the
california Legislature to attend its annual legislative meeting, at which legislative education and
issues of state and national policy are discusied. The scholarships are offered to reimburse the
legislators' travel and lodging for attendance at ALEC seminars and conferences. ALEC,s
national chairman appoints a california state legislator to serve as a public sector chair. The
state chair seryes as the head of the delegation ind has discretion to recommend, and without

california Legislature should be reimbursed for their travel and lodging expenses. Neither the

except for the one state public sector chair that makes recommendations to ALEC.

or meetings.
sections.analysis:
Making and Receipt of Gifts
The term "gift" is defined in Section g202g(a) as:
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration ofequal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."2
The Act regulates the receipt of gifts by legislators and specific legislative staff in four
ways that are relevant to your question.
2 Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any ..rendering of
whether tangible or intangible.
services or anything else of value,
o First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials.
certain state and local officials are prohibitld from accepting gifts from any single sollrce
in any calendar year with a total value of more rhan the gift limit. (Section gg503(a).)
The current gift limit, which is adjusted biennially each odd numbei year to reflect
changes in the Consumer price Index, is $420. (Regulation lgg4}.Z.i
o Second' the Act.prohibits lobbyists and lobbying firms from making gifts to a legislator
or specific legislative staff aggregating moreth; $10 in a calendar-nionth, or to act as an
agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift
by any other person. (Section 96203.)
o Third, so that the public is made aware of any potential undue influences from donors
who make gifts, the Act imposes reporting obligations requiring certain public officials to
disclose the source of any gifts aggiegating $50-or more in value. Section g7203
provides that "[e]very person who hoids an office specified in Section glzooshall . . . file
a statement disclosing his [or her] investments, his for her] interests in real property and
his [or her] income . . .." The term "income" includes any gift. (Section g2030.) Elected
State office-rs, including Members of the Legislature, are included in Sectio n g7200.
(Section 82024.)
' 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence the olltco-" oi u gou"*ri"ntal decision
involving the donor of a gift or gifts 
.w-ith an aggregate value of g420 or more provided to,
received by, or promised to the official withinff,e 12 months prior to the date the decision
is made. (sections 87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4;as to conflicts of
interest and members of the Legislature, ul.o ,"" Sections g7 lo2, g7 102.5 and g7 102.6.)
Notwithstanding the_general gift provisions explained above, Section g9506 controls
whether a gift of travel is subject to th" Act's gift limii, providing the following in pertinent part:
"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that ure r.uronubly related to a
legislative or go-v-ernmental purpose, or to an issue of state, nationar,"or
intemational public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of
the following apply:
*,ft
"(2)The travel is provided by a government, a govemmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private educatio"nal
institution, as defined in Section zo3 of the Revenue and raxation code, a
nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the
lnternal Revenue Code, or a by a person domiciled outside the united States
which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status under Section


File No. ,4-10-133
501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code."

QUESTION: I
Based Llpon the facts you have provided, the travel in question appears to be reasonably
related to an issue of state, national, or international public policy m trrai the travel will give
officials information on issues related to their work in the ialifornia Legislature. If the source of
any payment for the travel is ALEC, which is a nonprofit organization e.Ixempir.o- taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue code, the payment is not subject to the Act,s
$420 gift limit.3 If the true source of the gifts is not one or tne entities set forth in Section
89506(a)(2), the gift limit would apply.
Source of Gift
Notwithstanding, the-gift exception for travel payments set forth above, you have
indicated that the payments for the travel and lodging'*ill"o*, from ALEC,s scholarship fund,
which is ftrnded by private donors. Thus, yoll must determine whether the source of any
particular payment is ALEC or the donor making a payment to ALEC for the scholarship fund.
Recently amended Regulation t1g45 provides tlie Commission's rule for determining the source
of a gift and provides the following, in pertinent part:
"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a gift to an
official and is not acting as an intermediary.
"(b) Gifts through an lntermediary.
"(1) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 87210 or 87313,1f the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirictly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the foiowing conditions:
'(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party ro make a gift ro the official
"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment will be used
by the rhird party to make a gift to the official.
"(C) The person identifies the official to rhe third party as the intended
I while a payment for travel by a 501(cX3) nonprofit organization is nor subject to the g420 gift limit, any
payments totaling $50 or more in-a calendar year must be disclose-d as a reportable gift under Section g7203.
Moreover' the payment may bar the public oifi:y,-yn9^r"."ir"J irr.lir.n, t'rom making, participating in making,
or influencing government decisions affecting ALEC, if they were tr,i ,our." of the gift. Even if a gift of travel
fiom a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization is exlmpt from the $+zo giir lirit, the ofricial generaily must srilldisquarity
himself or herself from any governmental decision *itt o,easonoBly iJr"r".oble material financial effect on the
source of a gitt or gifts that aggregate to a value of $420 or more in it. iz-ronth period prior to his or her
participation in the government decision. (Section g7 l0O et .seq.)
beneficiary of the payment prior to the third party making rhe payment to the
official.
"(D) lhe third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to the third
party.
"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
plrrpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials.
"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official.,'
Based on your facts, the provision that seems most applicable to your first question would be
subdivision (E). Subdivision (E) focuses. on the "soll or primary;' purpose ior making a
payment, necessarily based on the objective facts and circumstances that evidence an intent of
the donor. In footnote 3 of the Commission Memorandum conceming the adoption of
Regulation 18945 (Amendment to Regulation 18945 - Source of Grfti tutarch'17, 2010), staff
provided the following continuum:
"For example,_a person may make a payment to a third person specifically for an
identified conference knowing all attendees will be state legislators and that the
payment will ultimately pay for the legislators' travel and accommodations. In
these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party should be
considered the source of the gifts.
tiu
"On the other hand, if a person makes a payment for a widely attended event,
there is little indication that the payment will be used to makl a gift to an official
even when the person knows or has reason to know that the offic-ial will attend the
event' In these circumstances, the person making the payment to the third party
should not be considered the source of a gift prouid"dio the official from the third
party hosting the event."
Based on this, if the donors to ALEC's scholarship fund know or have reason to know
that the sole or primary pllrpose of their donations is to make gifts to California legislators, then
the true sources of the scholarship funds dispersed by ALEC to the legislators are the donors to
the ALEC scholarship fund, and not ALEC. ALEC would be the intJrmediary "f ;h;;li;;.; 
--
It appears likely that at least some donors to the ALEC scholarship fund know or have
t Under Sections 872 l0 and 873 I 3, an intermediary of a gifi to an official is required to provide the
tbllowing specitic infbrmation to the officer: the name, address and business activity of both the intermediary and
donor. In addition, since the value of the giti in this case is Iikely to be a p.o ruro J;;i;";;nor's 
toraldonarion
to ALEC, it would also be helpful to provide the official with that value.
reason to know that their contributions will fund the costs associated with the attendance of
California legislators to ALEC events. As you stated, ALEC regularly provides reimbursements
for travel and lodging to members of the California Legislature it.ougtr itr scholarship fund and
your questions indicate that these funds are sometimes solicited by Catifornia legislators.
However, because ALEC is a large organization that provides schtlarships to public officials
from many states, the donors may not know or have no reason to know that their donations
would be solely or primarily used for scholarships to California legislators. In that case, ALEC
would be the source of the gift and not the individual donors to thJscholarship fund.
Question 2
In your second question, you ask what reporting conseqllences follow for a legislator if
scholarship funds are solicited and raised by a legislatoi for the legislator who solicited the
funds, for a legislator who receives such funds, and for a legislatoi who makes recommendations
to ALEC as a public sector chair regarding which legislators should receive the scholarship
funds' Subdivision (bXlXF) of Regulation 18945, "it"d obor", provides that when an official
solicits payment from a donor to a third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official, the
donor is the source of the gift to that official and the thirO party the intermJdiary if the official
receives a gift responsive to the solicitation within 12 monihs Lf *omng the soiicitation. (See
also Regulation 18945(dX3).) Therefore, to the extent the California telislator, or his or her
agent, solicits donations to the scholarship fund, and indicates that the Califomia legislator will
be the beneficiary, the donor is the source of the gift and ALEC is an intermediary.
In addition, depending upon what is communicated as part of the solicitation or between
the donor and ALEC in connection with the donation, subdivision (b)(l)(A)-(D) could*,r.
Under subdivision (b)(1XA)-(D), the donor is the source of the gifi io tir" r"jirtutor or other
benefitting legislators and ALEC the intermediary.s Finally, "u", if subdiviiions (bXlXA)-(D),
o1 G) do not apply, if the facts surrounding the solicitation and making of the donation are
similar to those analyzed under Question I above, subdivision (bxl)d) may apply and the donor
could be the source of the gift and ALEC the intermediary.
Also, Section 82015(bX2XBXiii) states that when a payment for a legislative,
governmental or charitable purpose is made "at the behest" of an elected offtial, that official is
required to file a report with his or her agency within 30 days detailing, among other things, the
source, amount and purpose of the payment once the amount or amoLlnts donated at the behest of
that official by a single source total to $5,000 or more in a calendar year. This is known as a
"behested payment report" and the Commission's Form 803 is used for that pulpose. The public
sector chair who makes recommendations to ALEC as to which legislators should receive the
scholarship funds should also file behested payment reports for thJpayments made by ALEC to
legislators due to his or her recommendations that reacl $5,000 orrnor". on these reports ALEC
' For example, under subdivision (bX I XC), if the donor sends a check to ALEC and communicates to
ALEC that the donation is to assist in sending that Califbrnia legislator, or any other Calitbrnia legislators, named or
ttn-named, to an ALEC event, the donor would be the source otihe giti to the legislators who benefit fiom the
payment' and ALEC the intermediary. (See Regulation 18945(bx2ifbr claritication of how an ofTicial can be
identifred so as to tri-eger application of these provisions.)
should be listed as the payor and the legislator who receives the recommended payment would
be the payee. It should also be noted that Legislators who receive gifts through the ALEC
scholarship fund may be disqualified from making, participating in making, or using their
official positions to influence the outcome of governmental decisions that involve the donors
who were sources of gifts to them. (Sections 87100, 87I02,87102.5 and 18702.6.)
Question 3
In response to your third question, because Regulation 18945 went into effect on June 10,
2010, any funds raised by ALEC prior to June 10, 2010, would only be subject to the prior
version of Regulation 18945.
Question 4
Finally, in response to your fourth question, legislators are permitted to use their
campaign funds to cover costs to attend ALEC's functions or meetings. The Act provides that
campaign funds may be used for expenditures associated with holding office if the expenditures
are reasonably related to a legislative or govemmental purpose. Expenditures which confer a
substantial personal benefit must be directly related to a political, legislative, or govemmental
purpose. (Section 89512.) Therefore, because attendance at ALEC's annual legislative meeting
is related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose legislators may use their campaign
funds to cover costs to attend ALEC's functions or meetings. Also, please note that legislators
should refer to Regulation 18421.7 for additional reporting guidelines when reporting
expenditures for travel and meals made with campaign funds.

CONCLUSION: l' If ALEC scholarship fund donors know or have reason to know that the sole or
primary purpose of their donations is to make gifts to California legislators the donations would
be reportable gifts to legislators from the donois to the fund and .u-"h donor,s pro rata share
would be subject to th-e Act's $420 gift limit. In such a transaction ALEC would be an
intermediary of the gift and would need to disclose the sources of the funds it used to make
scholarships and other information as specified below to the recipients of those scholarships. In
addition, the recipients. w_ould be requirid to report those donor, 
-o, ,or.".s of gifts on their
Statements of Economic Interests if the share oi the donation from any on" doro, was g50 or
more in a calendar year' The legislators may_also be disqualified from making, participating in
!*inq, or using their official positions to influence the outcome of govemmental decisions
involving those donors, as discussed below. However, you stated th;t ALEC is a large
organization that provides scholarships to public officiais in many states. If the donors making a
donation to ALEC do not know or have reason to know that theiidonations will be solely or
primarily used for scholarships to california legislators, then ALEC is the source of the gifts and
not the individual donors.
2' lf a california legislator solicits a donation to ALEC for scholarship funds and the .
donor knows or has reason to know that the purpose of the donation is to pay for a scholarship
for the legislator, the donor is the source of tire gift, and ALEC is an interm"oi*y. Also, as
explained below, depending on what is communicated between the donor and ALEC in
connection with the making of the donation, the donation could be a gift to the legislator or other
legislators who received a scholarship, making the donor the source of the gift and ALEC the
intermediary' In addition, regardlesJof the so'iicitation by the legislator, the donor could be the
xource of the gift and ALEC the intermediary as set forth in the analysis of euestion I above.
Finally, if, in response to the califomia legisiator's solicitation, a donor makes a donation or
several donations to lh: schorarship fund and the amount of the donations is $5,000 or more in a
calendar year, as explained below,lhe Iegislator must file a "behested payment report,, (Fppc

single donor reach the $5,000 threshold. A behested payment report should also be filed by the
legislator who makes recommendations to ALEC as to which califomia r.lirioto* should
receive the scholarships listing ALEC as the payor and the recipient legislaior as the payee when
the payments by ALEC reach the $5,000 tfrreitrtla.
3' Scholarship funds raised prior to June 10, 2olo,the effective date of amendments to
Regulation 18945, are not subject to the new version of that regulation.


You are the Republican Leader of the Califomia State Senate and are writing on behalf of
ALEC' ALEC is a nonprofit voluntary membership organization of over 1,700 state legislators
and nearly 300 private members, foundations and assoc'iations. ALEC i, o.gonir.d and exempt
as a charitable organization under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue code and has been
since 1975' The primary purpose of ALEC is to educate legislators and private sector members
. on the issues that affect state government and to develop solutions to those issues. The
organization engages in research, creation of model legislation and best practices for state

educational goals by publishing its findings-and sfonsoring educational seminars and
conterences.
ALEC has members and participation from all 50 states and all 99 state legislative

a particular purpose. From this fund ALEC offers scholarships at its own discretion to the
california Legislature to attend its annual legislative meeting, at which legislative education and
issues of state and national policy are discusied. The scholarships are offered to reimburse the

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 22, 2010 Honorable Dennis Hollingsworth State Capitol, Room 305 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-133 Dear Senator Hollingsworth: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. Will reimbursements for travel and lodging from the American Legislative Exchange Council's ("ALEC") scholarship fund in connection with ALEC's annual legislative meeting constitute gifts made to public officials even if ALEC is a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization? If so, are those payments reportable by ALEC, by the private donors who fund the scholarship fund, or both? 2. If ALEC scholarship funds are solicited and raised by a member of the California Legislature, what reporting consequences follow from that solicitation for that member and for a member who receives reimbursement for travel and lodging expenses from ALEC scholarship funds? Would the analysis change if the member involved in soliciting donations was also involved in making recommendations to ALEC as to which members of the California Legislature should receive ALEC scholarship reimbursements for travel and lodging for ALEC's annual legislative meeting? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-133 Page No. 2 3. Are any scholarship funds raised prior to June 10, 2010, the effective date of recently amended Regulation 18945, subject to the intermediary gift disclosure described in the new version of the Regulation? 4. May a legislator use campaign funds to cover costs to attend an ALEC function or meeting? CONCLUSIONS 1. If ALEC scholarship fund donors know or have reason to know that the sole or primary purpose of their donations is to make gifts to California legislators the donations would be reportable gifts to legislators from the donors to the fund and each donor's pro rata share would be subject to the Act's $420 gift limit. In such a transaction ALEC would be an intermediary of the gift and would need to disclose the sources of the funds it used to make scholarships and other information as specified below to the recipients of those scholarships. In addition, the recipients would be required to report those donors as sources of gifts on their Statements of Economic Interests if the share of the donation from any one donor was $50 or more in a calendar year. The legislators may also be
